# Inference Cost

Every question you ask an AI model costs energy. Not a metaphor — electricity, measured in watt-hours, paid by a data centre, somewhere.

**Literal meaning:** Inference is what happens when a trained AI model is actually used — when it generates a response to a prompt. The inference cost is the energy consumed in that process, multiplied across every query, by every user, every day.

**Origin:** The term is native to machine learning engineering, where inference (applying a trained model to new input) is distinguished from training (building the model). Its emergence as a public accountability concept followed the rapid scaling of AI usage from 2022 onward, when the number of daily queries to large language models began reaching the hundreds of millions — making cumulative inference cost, previously negligible, a structurally significant figure.

> The energy consumed each time an AI model generates a response — small per query, enormous in aggregate, and almost never disclosed.

**The Appeal:** Per query, inference costs are genuinely small — fractions of watt-hours for a typical exchange. This is not spin; it is accurate. The efficiency argument is real: AI can answer questions faster and at lower marginal cost than many human-staffed alternatives. Researchers and companies building on AI infrastructure have legitimate reasons to find this cost acceptable.

**The Friction:** The problem is aggregation and invisibility. At hundreds of millions of daily queries, small numbers compound into large ones — and [[Carbon Footprint of AI]] calculations almost never include them. Companies regularly publish training emissions; inference emissions are rarely disclosed. The user interface is designed to feel weightless: instant, frictionless, free. [[Externalized Costs]] — the environmental burden transferred to communities near data centres — make the weightlessness possible. Someone pays. It just is not the person typing.

**Why This Matters:** Inference cost introduces a new kind of environmental reading: *how often* matters as much as *how large*. A habit becomes infrastructure. The daily conversation with an AI assistant is not neutral; it is a recurring small claim on shared energy resources, invisible by design.

**See also:** [[Training Run]] · [[AI Energy Consumption]] · [[Carbon Footprint of AI]] · [[Externalized Costs]] · [[Greenwashing]]
